description: US unemployment insurance: eligibility rules and quirks, quitting with good cause by state, appeals process and deadlines, overpayment waivers, and worker misclassification. Verified 2026.
source: Social Security Act Titles III/IX/XII, FUTA 26 U.S.C. ch. 23, 20 CFR Part 625/650, USDOL UI comparison of state laws, state UI statutes, NELP misclassification research

triples.subject: unemployment insurance (UI)
triples.object: joint federal-state program paying weekly benefits to workers unemployed through no fault of their own; each state sets its own eligibility, amounts, and duration under federal minimums

triples.subject: UI eligibility
triples.object: sufficient earnings in the base period, being able and available for work, and actively seeking work each week claimed

triples.subject: base period
triples.object: first 4 of the last 5 completed calendar quarters before filing; most states offer an alternate base period (most recent 4 quarters) for workers who fail the standard test

triples.subject: UI benefit amount
triples.object: roughly half of prior weekly wages up to a state maximum; maximums range from $235/week (Mississippi) to over $1,000/week (Massachusetts, plus dependent allowances)

triples.subject: UI benefit duration
triples.object: 26 weeks in most states; shorter in some — Florida and North Carolina slide as low as 12 weeks depending on the state unemployment rate

triples.subject: quitting and UI
triples.object: voluntarily quitting disqualifies a claimant unless they quit with good cause; roughly half the states require the cause be connected to the work, others accept compelling personal reasons

triples.subject: good cause: work-related
triples.object: substantial pay cut (commonly 20%+), unsafe conditions reported to the employer, unpaid wages, discrimination or harassment reported and unresolved, drastic change in duties or hours

triples.subject: good cause: domestic violence
triples.object: the large majority of states allow benefits for quitting due to domestic violence (relocating for safety); documentation like a protective order or shelter letter helps

triples.subject: good cause: trailing spouse
triples.object: quitting to follow a military spouse's transfer qualifies in most states; following a civilian spouse's relocation qualifies in a smaller set (e.g., CA, NY, KS) and is disqualifying in many others

triples.subject: good cause: medical
triples.object: quitting for one's own illness or disability generally qualifies if the claimant asked for accommodation or leave first and is still able to do some work; quitting to care for an ill family member qualifies in only some states

triples.subject: good cause
triples.object: claimants usually must show they tried to preserve the job first — reporting the problem, requesting transfer or leave — before quitting; skipping this step is a common denial reason

triples.subject: misconduct disqualification
triples.object: fired workers get benefits unless the employer proves misconduct — willful disregard of the employer's interests; ordinary poor performance, inability, or a single good-faith error is not misconduct

triples.subject: misconduct
triples.object: the burden of proof is on the employer; being fired 'for cause' in HR language does not automatically mean UI-disqualifying misconduct

triples.subject: UI appeal
triples.object: strict and short: 10-30 days from the determination mailing date depending on state (e.g., 10 days in Missouri, 15 in Texas, 21 in New York, 30 in California); late appeals are dismissed absent good cause

triples.subject: UI appeal hearing
triples.object: informal hearing (usually by phone) before an administrative law judge or referee; both sides testify under oath, can submit documents and question witnesses; decisions come in writing

triples.subject: UI appeal
triples.object: claimants win a large share of appeals — especially quit/misconduct cases where the employer fails to appear; free legal aid or a claimant advocate substantially raises win rates

triples.subject: UI appeal levels
triples.object: determination → ALJ/referee hearing → board of review → state court judicial review; new evidence generally only enters at the hearing stage, so bring everything then

triples.subject: certifying during appeal
triples.object: keep filing weekly claims while any appeal is pending; winning the appeal only pays weeks that were properly certified

triples.subject: UI overpayment
triples.object: benefits the state later decides were not owed; non-fraud overpayments (agency error, reversed decisions) are waivable, while fraud overpayments carry penalties of 15%+ and disqualification weeks

triples.subject: overpayment waiver
triples.object: most states waive non-fraud overpayments when the claimant was without fault and repayment would cause financial hardship or be against equity and good conscience; a waiver must usually be requested — it is not automatic

triples.subject: overpayment
triples.object: appeal the overpayment determination itself and separately request a waiver; states can offset future benefits and tax refunds (TOP) but exempt income rules and payment plans apply

triples.subject: worker misclassification
triples.object: labeling employees as independent contractors (1099) to avoid UI taxes; misclassified workers can still file a UI claim, which triggers a state audit of the employment relationship

triples.subject: misclassification tests
triples.object: many states use the ABC test (worker is an employee unless free from control, outside the usual course of business, and independently established — e.g., CA under AB5, NJ, MA); others use common-law control factors

triples.subject: 1099 workers
triples.object: file the claim anyway and report the work relationship accurately; getting a 1099 does not decide the question — the state applies its own test to the facts

triples.subject: severance and UI
triples.object: treatment varies by state: some delay benefits during weeks covered by severance (e.g., TX, NY over a threshold), others (e.g., CA) do not count true severance against benefits at all

triples.subject: part-time earnings
triples.object: claimants can work part-time and keep partial benefits; states disregard a portion of earnings (a flat amount or fraction of the weekly benefit) and reduce the rest dollar-for-dollar

triples.subject: refusal of suitable work
triples.object: refusing a suitable job offer or referral can end benefits; suitability weighs prior wages, skill, commute, and length of unemployment — standards loosen the longer the claim runs

triples.subject: UI benefits and taxes
triples.object: UI benefits are federally taxable income; claimants can elect 10% federal withholding (Form W-4V) to avoid a surprise bill
